sentences.text: Yes, very much so.
After we were liberated, in the short period of time that we stayed in our original home, my father--
The farm.
Correct.
After the Russians liberated us, under the auspices of the Russians, my father, of course, knew where the people from the ghetto or the mass grave, if you will, was located.
And my father was instrumental in, in, in putting a fence around it, as well as building a monument.
And he literally built the monument with his own hands.
And I remember as a child taking part or being-- seeing the ceremony at the time that the-- they dedicated the monument.
And they-- of course, they had Russian soldiers there and whatever few people from that area, survivors and of course friends-- people that lived in the area were in attendance.
And I remember walking around with a young lady that eventually became my sister.
Aviva.
And my cousin was there and another young man.
I remember us walking around the area and seeing perhaps hair parts or pieces of clothing on the ground.
And I remember the young lady kind of seeing hair and thinking, oh, gee.
Maybe this was my mother's.
And I remember what we thought at the time, what looked like blood, seeping through the ground, because the grave was very shallow.
And this I remember specifically.
I also remember finding some kind of a stencil that we kind of fought over at the time.
And so that I remember specifically after the war, that, that, that particular incident.
We thought so.
I mean, of course, we were children, maybe four or five years old at the time.
And these are the thoughts that went through our minds and that I specifically remember being there and seeing things.
And of course, at that time, we, of course, were part of family discussions that took part-- of ways that my-- I also have an Uncle Max, my father's brother, who survived with his wife and son.
Also, they, they hid out in the woods during that whole time, actually dug holes in the woods.
And, and they survived also within that same area.
And of course, after the war we all stayed together and so forth.
And so certain family discussions and so forth would take place.
And of course, you would hear the various stories and information about how this person was killed and how that person was killed and so forth.
Always.
Always.
And there's still a lot of crying that takes place now whenever certain things are recalled by my father and the rest of my-- my uncle and so forth, when we get together.
The memories are still very much etched in, in their minds, of course, in my father's mind now.
Certain things.
I think part of it, what little I remember, certainly during the war everything certainly after the war, I think it makes you feel different.
It's kind of hard to put it into words.
But I think you feel Jewish, in a way, if you will, not in, in-- maybe in a bad way, it's just that you just feel different.
And certainly even being in the United States-- and I certainly have been fortunate to have a wonderful life in the United States and so on.
But I think you still feel a little different, as much as with the friends and people that you associate with.
I always call it kind of thinking in Jewish.
And as I talk to you, even though I talk English fairly well, I still do my thinking in Jewish.
It's, it's an unusual feeling.
Enough times I've talked with some of my friends about it and with my family, same way.
And they find it kind of difficult to believe that this is taking place.
And I think just as a result of all the experiences, you just can't help but feel and think differently than most other people.
Jewish and Polish was our household language.
I still speak Jewish very fluently, of course.
Polish, a little bit, but I've forgotten a great deal, primarily through not using it.
But I find that when I'm in the company of people that speak Polish, when I hear it spoken, it's still-- I can understand enough of it to get by.
I don't remember necessarily fear during the time of hiding, other than perhaps the crying part and being discovered.
Some of it might be just because I heard some of these things being discussed with my parents.

But I think after the war and from then on, I think there are certain fears-- certainly when I was a young child, I-- in, in a DP camp, as well as when we first came to the United States, I think I still have certain fears about being alone or left alone.
And my parents never left me alone.
I never had a babysitter after the war.
And even when we came to United States, my parents used to take me wherever they went, be it day or night.
So I guess those are some of the fears, for whatever reason.
I never really thought about fear itself, but there were certain fears, obviously.
I don't recall any specific ones, no.
Nothing that I really recall and so on.
In Germany, they placed us in a DP camp, again DP standing for Displaced Persons.
We were placed with other Jewish families at a camp in Pocking, spelled-- in Germany, called P-O-K-I-N-G.
If I recall, it was not very far from Munich.
It was within a short driving distance from Munich, so that kind of locates it where it was.
The D camp was formerly used as a German army base, if you will.
And they put everyone-- the barracks were still-- for the most part still standing, although I remember seeing rubble.
So some of them obviously were knocked down.
But I remember seeing various-- and playing with military hardware that was still there, and finding certain things like bullets and things and-- that presented themselves as good toys at the time.
And each family was given a room within the barracks to live in.
I remember just being-- having one faucet for water down the end of the hallway.
It was like a central hallway and there were rooms to either side of the hallway.
I don't-- there were no showers or anything like that.
The sanitary facilities were in another building on the other side of a lot away from the building that I guess was shared by other barracks as well.
I do remember that.
And I remember my father building a small hut next to the window of our room-- the room had one window-- where he would store other things, wood.
And he would also store chickens and, and, and geese and ducks and things, livestock, if you will, that they would feed and make fat.
And my mother would-- the rabbi would kill one, and she would cook one up occasionally and so on.
But I remember food being fairly plentiful at the time, being given-- you know, used to-- they used to give milk, a certain amount of milk for each child.
And they used to-- we used to stand in line and get bread.
And then my mother-- my father was able to procure-- he'd go out amongst the farmers and, and, and get chickens and, and and, and geese and so forth, so food was fairly plentiful.
I do recall getting care packages as well during that time, some of them having food in them.
I remember Spam was a very special thing.
When you got a can of Spam in one of those packages, that used-- my mother used to save that for a special occasion.
That was a real delicacy.
I remember getting some pencils and, and erasers in some of the care packages.
That was a very special treat as well.
As a matter of fact, I had an eraser up until the time I was in junior high school here in the United States.
Those are things that I remember.
Yes.
In one of the care packages.
And I had that up to-- I'm a saver, so I had that for quite a while.
I remember it through junior high school.
After that, I kind of lost track of it.
In that room, my mother and I'll call her my adopted sister Aviva and my father and I all lived in that one room.
And we had-- I don't know if it was a bed or whatever, a certain-- the place where we would sleep.
And then I remember there being a stove, and that was the means of heating the room, and a table.
And I remember my mother giving us baths in a small basin.
She would heat up the water in a-- in a-- on the stove, and we would get, I guess, like, a sponge bath in a basin.
Right.
We would bring the faucet-- the water from the faucet to our room, and this is the way we would get a bath.
I remember also at that time getting my first pair of shoes.
I remember that was-- that was actually made-- custom made for me by a cobbler.
I don't remember ever shopping in the store.
They used to-- mostly like the rags from potato sacks and so forth.
We had no socks.
I think that was-- again, even in the shoes, they would wrap the foot around with a cloth, and that's the way you-- I would wear the boots.
Specifically, no.
Yes.
My mother would do the cooking.
And it was-- in a way, it was one of the dreams coming true that my mother used to tell me the stories within the hiding in the hole.
What it would be like after the war, when we were liberated.
And that was one of things that would come true.
So.
Her cooking, exactly, and baking.
My mother was a very good cook and she would bake, and especially on Fridays before the Jewish Sabbath.
They were very observant and so forth.
And I also remember they, they had like a community oven in the camp where-- you know, during the Jewish Sabbath, you're not supposed to light fires and so forth.
So you can't cook warm food.
I remember as a little boy carrying [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what they used to call cholent.
It's a certain dish that my mother would put in a pot.
It was more like a stew.
And she would cover it up with paper.
And on a Friday afternoon, we would bring that to the community oven and that would be kept warm until the meal.
Saturday morning, of course, they would go to synagogue.
And after synagogue, they would have their meal, and we would pick up the stew, if you will, at the community oven and bring it home to eat.
During that time I remember that being very special.
No.
They-- as I mentioned, the bathroom facilities, sanitary facilities were located in a-- in a building nearby across from a-- I remember a field or an area.
That-- it was just a building.
And they would have a hole, a ditch dug underneath.
And they would have like wooden planks with holes cut into them.
And that was the-- that was-- those were the toilet facilities.
Exactly.
Exactly.
A community outhouse.
I remember.
Probably a couple of hundred feet across a-- an area.
I don't if it was like a parking lot, I would say, or a field, if you will.
I remember it was just-- there was no grass growing.
It was primarily sand and gravel.
Approximately-- well, 19-- end of '45 to 1949, until the time we emigrated to the United States.
Oh, gosh.
Five to, to nine.
Correct.
My father had a bicycle.
And during that time he was also-- near the camp there was like a-- I wouldn't call it a resort area, but where they had these special hot springs, sulfur springs.
And I remember they built like a swimming pool.
And they also had, like, bath-- different private baths there for people to come, I guess-- the people that were suffering from various diseases, that would help them.
And my father was made manager of that particular area.
And-- but my father made friends with surrounding farmers and so forth.
I guess that was his way.
And he was able to either trade, or buy, or barter for additional food and so forth.
Exactly.
It was run by the local-- so forth, under the auspices of the, you know, American government or the-- that was, you know-- again, we could come and go.
The DP camp, we were free.
You could come and go anytime you wanted to and so forth.
And I, I also remember occasion where we would be put-- we'd go on the back of an army truck to Munich for my mother and father to do shopping or whatever.
I remember.
A US Army truck, exactly, and so forth.
And another area where I remember specifically they had like a-- like a carnival that came to the area.
And my father somehow had American Camel cigarettes.
And that was, I guess, a big commodity.
And he would kind of bribe one of the-- I used to like the dodge-em cars.
And he would give the guy a pack of cigarettes, and he would let me ride for hours for that.
I remember that specifically as well.
Yes.
There were other children.
And we had school, a Hebrew school, if you will, that we had to attend every day.
And I remember, you know, playing stickball and, and other things with other children.
Soccer was a big thing in those days as a child.
And we would learn Hebrew and math as well.
It was like a regular school.
I remember attending that.
I remember learning how to ride the bike.
I was quite small, and it was a man's bike with the bar and I actually couldn't reach over the bar, so I learned how to ride underneath the bar.
That's how I learned how to ride a bike.
Teachers were primarily rabbis and most of the teaching was, you know, Hebrew.
And I remember some math.
And I remember one rabbi specifically.
He had a long, black beard.
And, and occasionally he'd come to the house and complain about my misbehavior.
And my father would give him something as a tip for doing it, so he used to like doing that.
But--
I would get a beating.
My father.
I'd get disciplined.
Not that I recall.
I just remembered having to say the morning prayers, and the afternoon prayers, and evening prayers every day.
And I remember math.
And I guess I was a fairly good student as well.
Yes.
There were quite a few.
I'd say uncertainty in the thousands.
And occasionally, they would have like demonstrations, being pro-Israel or pro-Palestine at the time.
And again, they would have different organizations, one of which was called the Betar.
And kind of, we would dress up almost like Boy Scouts here in the United States.
That's the best way to describe the way we would look.
And it was primarily a Zionist type of an organization, pro-Israel and so forth.
And they would be educating us and being for Israel or Palestine at the time, again.
Adults as well were organized and-- at times.
We have-- I have pictures of my father, everybody being lined up in a marching position, if you will, holding various signs and so forth.
I guess we would call them a demonstration for Israel.
Definitely.
Yes.
I remember a lot of children.
As far as being crowded, I don't remember, you know, thinking that way.
We were taught in Hebrew, and, of course, Jewish and Polish.
Those were the primary languages at that time.
And being in a German sector, I think German started coming into the language as well, the usage of the language.
Certainly my father learned it, having-- being more exposed to the people outside the camp.
And I, as a young man, apparently picked up some of it too, to a point where I can speak German fairly well now still.
And I reviewed it somewhat in, in high school and college by taking German as a language.

The primary language at that time was I'd say Polish and, and, and Yiddish, Jewish, primarily because of the, the adopted sister Aviva speaking only Polish at that time and learning a little bit of Jewish.
So because of her, we would speak Polish amongst ourselves.
Right.
During the time that we were hiding in the-- in the-- in the holes, she was with a Gentile family.
So from age-- she was less than-- under two years old to almost age four.
You know, so during that time, of course, she learned the language, so she spoke strictly Polish.
She was really my best friend.
You know, we hung out together all the time.
Oh, anything and anything.
Whatever we would find.
Sometimes we would find a piece of war material that we would find-- would amuse us.
So we'd play with that.
We would occasionally go to a movie.
They would have-- show movies.
I don't recall what type of movies they were, but I remember going.
I even remember going-- the two of us going together to a movie and we got home late.
And we, we got punished by getting a, a whipping.
And I remember my parents kind of, I, I hate to say, feeling sorry for her.
So I would get double.
I would get it for both of us, the whipping.
But I remember that coming home late, and we really got it badly that time.
In the camp, in the DP camp, in a-- in a separate building somewheres on the grounds.
Correct.
It's hard to say.
There were maybe [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 15 or 20 rooms on both sides of a long hallway.
Every room had a different family.
And I remember one family being across from us.
Their last name was Nudler, N-U-D-L-E-R.
And there was a father and a daughter and two sons.
And the sons were maybe in their 20s, and the daughter as well.
And I remember they were our closest friends in the camp.
And I remember one of the brothers especially.
His name was Moshe, who lives in Canada now, was especially kind of like a mentor to me.
And I remember being very fond of him.
And I guess he was fond of me as a little boy.
We still stay in contact now, of course.
And I remember my, my uncle and aunt and their son were in a different camp.
That's my father's brother.
And of course, my father's sister, Bunya, who hid out with us, she got married in the camp immediately or very close to the time that we arrived there.
And she lived with her husband and she had one child.
And she also lived in the camp.
She did not live in the same barrack that I-- as I recall.
In a different barrack.
My Aunt Bunya immigrated to Israel just a short period before we left for the United States.
And she took with her my sister Aviva.
Again, she delivered her, so to speak, to her aunt in Israel at the same time.
And I remember the time that we were separated and so forth.
